data.title: Fortum Oyj 2025 Q2 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Fortum Oyj in conjunction with their 2025 Q2 earnings call.

data.title: SRVR: Strong Data Infrastructure Trends But Poor Fund
data.description:
The economy is becoming increasingly digital, and A.I. / Cloud technologies are fostering important needs for digital infrastructure.

The Pacer Data & Infrastructure Real Estate ETF (NYSEARCA:SRVR) is at the center of this new trend, I give it a hold rating.

The fund benefits from the exposure to a fast growing industry, with positions in data centers and telecom REITs that should see a volume of $1.7 Tn global spending through 2030.

However, the portfolio concentration, valuation metrics and track record are tempering my optimism, preventing me from giving it a buy rating. I think there are better options out there.

The Data Surge

Once a niche, digital infrastructure is now a central area of focus for the global economy: you recall the $1.7 Tn investment volume from the introduction. It is the main driver of the increase of IT spending: +7.9% in 2025, reaching $5.4 Tn, driven by software and data centers.

SRVR is tracking the performance of the Solactive GPR Data & Infrastructure Real Estate Index and holds 24 companies with a significant 75.16% concentration on the top 10. The top 3 alone represent a concentration of 42%.

Looking at the sector breakdown, the fund focuses on real estate (59.90%), communication services (23.82%) and information technology (13.27%).

Key holdings are:

  • Equinix (EQIX), global REIT leader in the data center industry. The demand is strong and the vacancy rates are going down.

  • American Tower (AMT), global network of communication infrastructure like cell towers needed for 5G and edge computing. The business is somewhat stable with long-term lease agreements signed with telecom operators.

  • Digital Realty (DLR), operator of data centers focused on renewable energy (75% of the energy consumption).

As a peer, I will mention the iShares U.S. Digital Infrastructure and Real Estate ETF (IDGT), with a portfolio of 25 companies slightly less concentrated as the top 10 account for 68.63% of the fund. IDGT is focused on communications equipment (31%), digital infrastructure REITs (31%) and semiconductors (11.54%).

I will also mention, with a close positioning, the Global X Data Center & Digital Infrastructure ETF (DTCR). The fund is also significantly focused on its top 10 holdings with a 70.46% concentration for 25 holdings. The fund has a 56% position in real estate, close to that of SRVR and a 40% weight in IT, higher than SRVR with a diluted focus on communication services. The key difference stands in valuation metrics, which we will see below. I previously covered DTCR with a buy rating here, in the introduction when I say that there are better options, I’m thinking about this one.

Financial Metrics

The fund’s expense ratio is above peers, with 0.55%. IDGT stands at 0.39% and DTCR at 0.50%. This higher expense ratio is rationalized by the fact that the fund is bigger, and thus benefits from a larger liquidity: $437.9 Mn of AUM against $117.8 Mn for IDGT and $362.6 Mn for DTCR.

SRVR offers a dividend of 1.18% despite its 30-day SEC yield of 2.31% that suggests little to no problem with its sustainability. The two other peers are aligned with a dividend yield between 1.40% and 1.50%, despite lower 30-day SEC yields for both (1.49% and 1.74%).

In the section above I mentioned valuations, SRVR and IDGT are close with P/E ratios of 43.83 and 41.72 respectively. This is huge compared to the valuation of DTCR with a P/E ratio of 23.99. Let’s look at the performance to see if the higher multiples are justified with higher growth figures.

This is a cold shower for SRVR, as it consistently underperforms peers, and it is not a close match. The fund has not created value over the last 3 years, when peers have delivered double digits growth.

This is the performance as of June, the fund lost an additional 1% in July.

Sector Tailwind

The main driver of the REIT industry now is the increasing use of data linked with A.I. and cloud markets. JP Morgan projects a broad increase of REIT earnings of 3% in 2025 and 6% in 2026, providing a 10% total return. Data centers are contributing to this growth, as for 2025, their earnings are growing by more than 20%. The market for data centers, now worth $270Bn will reach $585 Bn in 2032, exhibiting a CAGR of 11.7%

As the fund is heavily positioned on data centers, with companies such as Equinix and Digital Realty, this is an important source of growth.

SRVR has a criterion of 85% of revenues coming from data/infrastructure activities, ensuring that all holdings are aligned with this trend. This is a differentiating element with peers, as it prevents the fund from holding American depositary receipts like IDGT or large cap companies like Nvidia for DTCR.

Risks

The fund is highly concentrated on top holdings, especially the top 3. This is a risk, as it ties the performance of the top 3 holdings with the performance of the whole fund (they almost account for half of the fund). This also introduces a high degree of company specific risk, like the regulatory scrutiny over AMT’s central role in the economy, which is exposed to monopoly regulations.

Power shortages can represent a risk of operating efficiency. The power consumption is surging now and putting pressure on infrastructure. Gartner estimated that by 2027, 40% of data centers will suffer from the energy availability. This also is linked to the necessity of having efficient power grids. I have an article dedicated to smart grid solutions here, that you might want to look at.

The macroeconomic environment represents a risk for two reasons. The focus on real estate comes with a high dependency on borrowing costs, and the market is now pricing rate cuts by the end of the year, which could, if delayed, have a negative impact on the fund. The other reason is the high P/E ratio of the fund, meaning that interest rates also have an effect on the net present value of portfolio companies, and if rate cuts are delayed, this will affect valuations adversely.

Conclusion

The sector in which SRVR is operating is supported by very strong tailwinds. However, I don’t find the fund to be very convincing after having looked at peers: it is the most expensive one, both in terms of annual expense and in terms of valuations, and this is not justified by the fund’s performance.

I will not rate it as a sell because the underlying industry is very strong, but I definitely don’t recommend buying it when much better options can be found.

data.title: Ulta Beauty Isn't Looking So Beautiful Anymore (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
In late August of last year, I decided to dig into Ulta Beauty, Inc. (NASDAQ:ULTA), the largest beauty retailer in the US. From the locations that it has in operation, it offers up cosmetics, fragrances, skin care products, and hair care products. The business even provides salon services to its customers. At the time, I argued that the business remained strong, exhibiting growth, having excess cash, and buying back shares. On top of this, I viewed the valuation of the company as being attractive. In response to these conditions, I decided to rate it a ‘buy’. And since then, things have gone quite well. Shares are up 51.3%, easily outperforming the 15% rise that the S&P 500 has seen. Today, however, I am making the difficult decision to downgrade the stock. Even though operational performance remains strong and the long term outlook for growth is positive, the business is no longer the bargain that it once was.

Of course, my assessment of the enterprise could change as new data becomes available. And it just so happens that, on August 28th, management will be reporting financial results for the second quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. Leading up to that point, analysts expect revenue to increase. But overall profitability is forecasted to decline. That's not terribly surprising considering how net profits have been as of late. Even as the business continues to grow from a revenue standpoint, bottom line results have been mixed, though they have tilted more in the negative direction. If we do see some major change on this front, I could perhaps upgrade it again. But for now, I believe that downgrading it to a ‘hold’ is appropriate.

Not so beautiful anymore

Before we get into the most updated data, which would be the first quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, I do think it would be helpful, considering how much time has lapsed, to look at how the company performed in 2024 compared to 2023. Overall sales increased nicely, climbing from $11.21 billion to $11.30 billion. Even though the company experienced comparable store sales growth of only 0.7% during this time, with the average ticket increase of 1.1% offset to some extent by a 0.4% drop in the number of transactions, the number of stores that it has in operation grew from 1,385 to 1,445.

While the increase in revenue for the company was nice to see, profitability declined, with net income dropping from $1.29 billion to $1.20 billion. Some of this was because of a drop in the firm's gross profit margin from 39.1% to 38.8%. This was primarily because of a contraction in merchandise margins that seems to have been caused by increased promotional activity, as well as other issues like higher supply chain costs and the expenses associated with new store openings. Selling, general, and administrative costs, rose from 24% of sales to 24.9%. This was associated with greater store payroll and benefits costs, and additional strategic investments that the company was making. Other profitability metrics fell as well. Operating cash flow, as an example, dropped from $1.48 billion to $1.34 billion. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a decline from $1.96 billion to $1.79 billion. And finally, EBITDA for the company fell from $1.92 billion to $1.83 billion.

*Average Price & # of Transactions are components of Comparable Store Sales

For the 2025 fiscal year, the only data we currently have involves the first quarter. During that time, revenue came in at $2.85 billion. That represents an increase of 4.5% year over year. A good chunk of this was the result of a rise in comparable store sales of 2.9%, with the company benefiting to the tune of 2.3% from higher prices and to the tune of 0.6% from higher transactions. The company also saw an increase to 1,451 stores compared to the 1,395 stores it had one year earlier.

Even though revenue increased, profitability for the company contracted, with net income falling from $313.1 million to $305.1 million. Some of this was the result of a small drop in gross profit margin from 39.2% to 39.1%, with management attributing that to supply chain fixed costs and store deleveraging, as well as a reduction in other miscellaneous revenue. But the big driver was a rise in selling, general, and administrative costs, from 24.4% of revenue to 24.9%. This was because of deleveraging of store payroll and benefits costs, as well as store expenses. A lot of this seems to be tied to the company's growth plans. Other profitability metrics, however, actually came in strong. Operating cash flow jumped from $159.3 million to $220 million. On an adjusted basis, the increase was more modest from $472.5 million to $484.5 million. And finally, EBITDA for the company expanded from $465.7 million to $473.8 million.

*Average Price & # of Transactions are components of Comparable Store Sales

When it comes to the 2025 fiscal year in its entirety, management anticipates revenue of between $11.5 billion and $11.7 billion. Most of this will come from the addition of 60 net new stores. This, combined with the remodeling of between 40 and 45 stores, can be expected to cost shareholders between $425 million and $500 million in the form of capital expenditures. Of course, that also includes money that is allocated toward other basic capital initiatives. So not all of it is growth-oriented.

Earnings per share should be between $22.65 and $23.20. At the midpoint, that would mean net profits of $1.04 billion. That would be down from the $1.20 billion reported last year. But this does not factor in the $900 million in share buybacks the company is planning for the year, which will reduce the overall share count and implies a reduction in net profits even greater than what my calculation shows. In all likelihood, these figures will mean adjusted operating cash flow of about $1.79 billion and EBITDA of $1.67 billion. If we use these estimates, and we also use the historical results for the company from last year, we can see in the chart below how shares are currently priced. The stock is certainly not expensive. But is no longer as cheap as it was when I looked upon it favorably last year.

There are some other things to take into consideration when deciding whether or not to buy into Ulta Beauty. This includes recent developments like the news that broke on August 14th that Ulta Beauty and Target Corporation (TGT) have decided to not renew their existing relationship that concludes in August of next year. Since launching in 2021, Ulta Beauty has grown to have over 600 shop-in-shop locations throughout Target stores. And the goal was eventually to grow this to 800. It sounds as though Target will continue to sell its products within its stores. But at the end of the day, we don't know exactly what kind of impact this will have on the company and its fundamental performance.

Just as there have been negative developments, there have been positive ones as well. On July 10th, management announced that the company had acquired Space NK Limited, a major British beauty retailer that has 83 stores spread throughout the UK and Ireland, as well as a growing online presence. But unfortunately, we don't know the terms of that arrangement or what impact it will have on the company either.

Hopefully, we will be getting some additional clarity when management announces results for the second quarter of the 2025 fiscal year on August 28th. Analysts currently anticipate revenue of $2.66 billion. That would represent an increase over the $2.55 billion reported one year earlier. However, earnings per share are expected to contract from $5.30 to $4.98, taking overall net profits down from $252.6 million to $226.6 million. In the table below, you can also see other profitability metrics for the second quarter of last year. Even though it would be easy to say that a drop in profits will lead to a drop in cash flows also, that is not a guaranteed thing. In the first quarter of this year, a decline in profitability was met with an increase in cash flows. But I do think investors would be wise to keep an eye on those metrics when the news comes out.

Long term, I will say that I am optimistic about the business. In 2024, the industry was already large, totaling $118 billion in the US alone. That was up materially from the $89 billion it stood at back in 2019. This large market is fueled by 140 million beauty enthusiasts aged 13 and older. What's more, management predicts that the space will grow at a low to mid single digit rate each year through 2027. This certainly offers additional upside for investors in the long run, even though I don't think that upside will be enough to justify further market-beating returns.

Takeaway

Right now, I still believe that the future for Ulta Beauty is bright. But I don't think it necessarily makes for a beautiful prospect. Profit and cash flow issues over the last couple of years have been problematic. The stock is also nowhere near as cheap as it once was. Yes, it does have a massive net cash position of $454.6 million that provides it with significant stability and flexibility. But that's not enough to prevent me from downgrading it from a ‘buy’ to a ‘hold’.

data.title: Raise Your Retirement Income With Up To 9.4% Yield
data.description:
Co-authored with Hidden Opportunities

In Seinfeld, George’s panic over “shrinkage” after a swim in cold water was harmless. In retirement, though, shrinkage is far more serious. Many retirees have looked back with regret after blowing through their savings too quickly or underestimating the rising cost of living and healthcare. Following the 4% rule or a similar drawdown strategy can feel like a race against your lifetime clock – every year, a little more disappears, and market volatility can feel like a rollercoaster. It’s stressful, and worse, you may leave behind nothing for your next of kin. That’s why we prefer building income-focused portfolios that let you live comfortably while preserving your capital, rather than watching it steadily shrink away.

At High Dividend Opportunities, we hold a wide range of income-producing securities to ensure the consistency of our income stream across market conditions. This includes fixed-income investments that offer steady payments while insulating us from market noise and volatility, and dependable companies that prioritize dividend payouts are supported by steady, recurring revenues. We continue to snag bargain pricing amidst elevated rates, while being compensated for waiting for the upside from rate cuts. Today, we will discuss two well-managed securities that deliver steady monthly dividends to their shareholders.

Pick #1: PFFA – Yield 9.4%

Virtus InfraCap U.S. Preferred Stock ETF (PFFA) presents one of the finest ways to invest in publicly traded preferreds while collecting generous monthly dividends. PFFA had its IPO in May 2018, and since then, the ETF has made 85 monthly dividend payments, amounting to $14.71/share. Given PFFA’s IPO price was $25/share, that is a spectacular 58.8% return on the investment in the form of usable cash, without any reduction in ownership. Over the past five years, PFFA has delivered annual dividend increases, with its current $0.17/share monthly payout yielding 9.4% annually.

PFFA’s active management helps the fund navigate two common risks faced by fixed-income securities – credit risk and call risk. Moreover, financial services companies are typically the largest issuers of preferred securities, and the preferred stock market indices, such as the S&P U.S. Fixed Rate Preferred Stock Index, maintain a disproportionate allocation to this sector. This makes them vulnerable to sector-specific headwinds, such as the regional bank failures of 2023 or catastrophic losses faced by insurance companies after major natural disasters. Source

PFFA maintains a more balanced sector allocation, mitigating the risk of sector concentration. However, depending on sector sell-offs, the ETF has been known to aggressively seize opportunities by modifying its allocation levels. Source

Notably, the fund holds several of the preferred stocks and baby bonds we hold in our portfolio, with a 70% overlap in its top ten holdings (including our bonus picks).

As a cash machine, PFFA keeps growing rapidly, with $1.7 billion in AUM.

The fund operates with ~20% leverage, which carries floating rates, as indicated by its weighted average interest rate of 5.51% for the first six months of its fiscal year. This positions the ETF well in the event of rate cuts, as it would make its underlying assets more valuable and experience lower borrowing costs, paving the way for dividend increases.

Pick #2: O – Yield 5.6%

Realty Income Corporation (O) is the largest triple-net-lease REIT, which ended its second quarter with 15,600 properties (346 million sq ft of leasable space), leased to 1,630 clients across 91 industries. The eREIT ended Q2 with 98.6% occupancy and a rent recapture rate across 346 leases of 103.4% (comprising both renewals and new leases). Notably, the REIT secured a 139% recapture rate on re-leases to new clients, indicating strong market rent growth.

During Q2, O invested $1.2 billion at a weighted-average cash yield of 7.2%, with 74% of these investments made in the European Union. Management cited that EU markets have a fragmented competitive landscape, offering a greater Total Addressable Market than the U.S. and more favorable borrowing costs. The company took advantage of this and issued €650.0 million of 3.375% senior unsecured notes due June 2031, and €650.0 million of 3.875% senior unsecured notes due June 2035. As of Q2, the EU accounts for 17% of O’s ABR (Annualized Base Rent), and the REIT expanded into its 8th EU country during the quarter, with a €250 million sale-and-leaseback with one of Europe's largest window manufacturers. Due to the strong momentum in acquisitions, the company is raising the 2025 investment guidance to ~$5.0 billion.

During Q2, O also sold 73 properties for total net proceeds of $117 million ($100 million was related to vacant properties). As of June 30, O reported ample liquidity of $5.1 billion, with manageable maturities through 2026. O is the only net lease REIT with an A3-rated balance sheet, and the aforementioned note issuances at low coupons are indicative of the company’s strong balance sheet and financial strength.

The impact of these acquisitions will not be seen immediately, and management commented that they will be realized in 2026. During Q2, the REIT reported net income of $195.9 million ($0.22/share) and AFFO of $1.05/share. O is also raising the low-end of its AFFO per share guidance to a range of $4.24 to $4.28/share, placing its dividend at a 76% payout ratio at the midpoint.

Conclusion

The sitcom version of shrinkage gets laughs – the retirement version does not. Selling off your portfolio to fund your lifestyle in retirement may work for a while, but market downturns, inflation, and rising medical bills can make the decline frighteningly fast. The last thing you want to be worried about in your golden years is outliving your savings.

Many retirees who thought that they had “enough” found themselves scrambling later, or leaving little behind for their loved ones. With a portfolio consisting of income-generating investments like O and PFFA, you can flip the script: enjoy steady monthly dividends, eliminate the need to time the market to sell assets, and keep your portfolio intact and ready to serve your loved ones as it served you. This is the power of our Income Method, enabling you to live fully and leave behind a lasting legacy.

data.title: I Liked UnitedHealth At $240, And I Like It At +$300
data.description:
The Warren Buffett effect is here:

UnitedHealth Group (NYSE:UNH) is ripping in pre-market trading on Friday, up over 12% after Warren Buffett's Berkshire Hathaway disclosed a position of 5 million shares, valued at $1.57 billion.

This vote of confidence comes at a time when UNH is in desperate need of it, with the stock down over 46% year to date, being at the forefront of investors' scrutiny. Things are about to change with the market open on Friday, consistent with my last buy rating on UNH at $240, and the only way now is up.

Before I get into it, here’s the timeline of events that led up to this:

  • Dec 2024: Stock last traded above $600 before the fatal shooting of Brian Thompson, CEO of the medical insurance division, in Manhattan, sparking public outrage and increased scrutiny of the industry.
  • Late Dec 2024: Downturn begins after UNH warns of rising medical expenses and issues a cautious FY25 EPS forecast (~$30).
  • Jan 2025: Company reiterates full-year forecast
  • Apr 2025 (Q1 earnings): Guidance cut to $24.65–$25.15 EPS (from $28.15–$28.65).
  • May 2025: CEO Andrew Witty resigns for personal reasons; FY25 outlook withdrawn. Shares drop over 18% to a four-year low.
  • May 2025: Former CEO Stephen Hemsley (2006–2017) was brought back to restore confidence, but the market views it as a short-term fix.
  • Aug 2025 (Q2 earnings): Stock falls another 7% after weaker-than-expected 2025 guidance, EPS now expected at $16 vs. $20.9 consensus; revenue outlook $445.5 billion–$448 billion vs. $449.1 billion consensus.

Past ratings and how these worked so far:

I initiated UNH with a buy back in late May, when it was trading at $297. The stock is set to open above $300 this morning, with investors poised to make gains on my initial recommendation to add UNH on an attractive valuation as the market reaction (to me) looked disconnected from the company’s long-term earnings power, ability to navigate curses and operational scale. Fast forward to late July, when I maintained the stock with a buy pre-2Q earnings at $283. While valuation was (and remains) at historically low multiples, my buy was also on the company’s fundamentals, especially its diversified revenue model, driven by Optum’s expanding role in data analytics, pharmacy services, and value-based care, providing UNH with a lasting advantage in an increasingly digital and cost-conscious healthcare system.

And last but not least, recommending investors to buy at the bottom (or near it), upgrading to a strong buy in early August post 2Q earnings, despite short-term headwinds including elevated medical costs, regulatory scrutiny, and Optum Health's revenue decline.

Investment thesis:

Going forward, despite the run we’ll see with market open, investors still need to see the following: an increase in customer spend and new customers. This goes hand in hand with margin stabilization. For now, free cash flow remains robust, which tells me one thing: this is a bruised stock, but not a dying one. Also, management is expecting inpatient utilization to make up a bigger portion of pressure on medical costs for 2025. The key factor now is whether the medical expense ratio can stabilize as the company adjusts its premiums. I’m encouraged that management seems focused on addressing their core challenge, restoring margins, but the real test is whether they can bring the ratio back in line with the 81% median.

I’m downgrading the stock to a cautious buy above $300. While I maintain my optimism in the long-term, I also see near-term gains from institutional and hedge fund buying behavior, shown below.

Trump’s big pharma deals could significantly reduce UNH’s drug procurement costs, driving its Medical Cost Ratio (MCR) down toward 80%, a level that I expect would meaningfully expand margins and boost EPS. With multiple positive catalysts aligning and institutional investors starting to take notice, I believe the market is underestimating the speed and scale of UNH’s potential profitability rebound. So, to keep it short, I do see upside ahead, especially as valuation multiples remain deeply under historical averages, but I also think there will be a lot of profit taking after the market open surge. I advise investors to hold the bag and add cautiously, as the $300 level will be one of many that UNH will overtake.

Let’s get to the stock’s valuation and technicals

If earnings forecasts change in the future, valuation metrics will move again, but purely from the price gap below is the proportional effect.

Forward valuation metrics

Pre-market price $271.4

Estimated post-market open price $300

Change

P/E Non-GAAP

16.74

18.4

10.5%

P/E GAPP

18.34

20.2

10.5%

EV/Sales

0.68

0.75

10.5%

EV/EBITDA

11.68

12.9

10.5%

EV/EBIT

13.95

15.4

10.5%

Price/Sales

0.55

0.6

10.5%

Even at current projected levels, I think UNH is still extremely undervalued. A stock, once at over $600, is now less than half that, pricing in a worst-case scenario while ignoring the company’s structural strengths, cost-cutting potential, and dominant market position. The stock closed at $217.4 on Thursday, but pre-market action on Friday is pushing it to $300.5, up around 11%, gapping significantly at the open. EMA21 at $266 will cross above EMA50 at $292, and now the next technical milestone is the EMA21 breaking above the EMA200 at $400.6. Still, this is a $135 gap, so I don’t expect it to happen immediately, but after months of a downtrend, the EMA21 slope is rising up, and sharply. I think a sustained price action above $300 will accelerate the timing of the bullish cross in the coming months, or even weeks. Once the stock opens above $300, the immediate support would be the EMA50, the first retest level on pullbacks. Resistance levels to watch are the $321-$325 levels, which are prior consolidation zones in May, while the longer-term resistance is at EMA200.

Risks and what’s next:

I think the biggest near-term risk is that today’s gap-up becomes a “sell the news” event, certainly more if short-term traders decide to lock in quick gains. The company also continues to work through elevated medical costs, and any surprise in utilization trends could pressure margins again. Operationally, while Berkshire’s stake is a strong vote of confidence, execution risk remains. The company also needs to prove that Optum’s slowdown is temporary and restore confidence in the long-term potential. That said, I see the current set-up as a multi-leg recovery rally if UNH can hold above $300 in the coming sessions and absorb profit taking.

I hereon share my sentiment on UNH and why I remain bullish on the stock at $300.

data.title: Energy Transfer Down Significantly From 2025 Highs - Opportunity For Income Investors
data.description:
If there is one debate that doesn't need to occur anymore it's whether we will need fossil fuels by the end of the decade. The narrative has completely changed, and more people are realizing that the demand for energy continues to increase, especially in traditional forms. The population continues to grow, automation continues to expand, and the number of data centers needed to support the future technological goals continuously increases. While commodity pricing can impact the supermajors that are focused on upstream production, the amount of energy flowing through America's energy infrastructure continues to expand which is why I am bullish on midstream operators, especially Energy Transfer (NYSE:ET).

Shares of ET reached $21.45 at the beginning of the year and over the past several months they have declined -18.97% to $17.38, which has pushed the distribution yield to 7.55%. I am very bullish on ET as their operational volumes increased in Q2 and their footprint is critical to the electric grid as there are direct connections to 185 gas fired power plants within its system. Roughly 90% of ET's earnings are protected by fee-based contracts which provides tremendous stability for income investors. Units of ET continued to sell-off after Q2 earnings because Co-CEO Thomas E. Long disclosed that on the earnings call that ET expects to come in either at or slightly below their Adjusted EBITDA guidance range of $16.1 billion to $16.5 billion. This is because of weakness in the Bakken region and slower recovery in several dry gas areas.

On the surface hitting the lower end of guidance or not meeting guidance is never a good sign, but as a long-term investor in ET I am looking at this period of weakness as a buying opportunity. The reality is that the demand for energy is going to continue to grow and that with all the projects ET is being brought online over the next several years, they are likely to continue their Adjusted EBITDA and distributable cash flow (DCF) expansion in the future which can lead to a higher valuation and a larger distribution. I plan on adding to my position in ET as I feel it's one of the best companies in the midstream sector.

Following up on my previous article about ET

Since my last article was published about ET in the middle of May (can be read here) its unit price has declined by -4.19% while the S&P 500 has increased by 9.45%. When the distribution is taken into account ET's total return over this period was -2.36%. I had felt that ET was positioned to benefit from surging energy demand driven by data centers, AI, and digital infrastructure expansion in the U.S. as its infrastructure was critical for upstream producers to move their products around the country. I was bullish on ET's ability to generate long-term capital appreciation and income going forward as they have a large backlog of projects expected to come online over the next several years. I am following up with a new article to discuss why the recent announcement that ET may miss their Adjusted EBITDA guidance range is nothing for long-term investors to worry about as I believe there is a lot of long-term value to be unlocked.

Risks to investing in ET

I am very bullish on ET but there are several risk factors for investors to consider and everyone should do their own due diligence. ET has over $59 billion in long-term debt on its balance sheet which makes them vulnerable to unfavorable macroeconomic environments especially if the Fed needs to raise interest rates. If interest expenses increase for ET it could divert its profitability away from the distribution and ET may not be able to provide their previous levels of annualized increases. While roughly 90% of ET's earnings are protected by fee-based contracts there are still commodity price risks tied to the remaining 10% of their earnings.

ET operates in a heavily regulated sector and lawsuits over environmental permits or unexpected failures of its pipelines could negatively impact their operations. There is also a risk that the data center build outs that are occurring slow down and that could cause the future projections to be wrong about the amount of energy needed and ET may end up allocating a lot of capital on projects that won't generate the intended return. Investors should do a lot of research and make sure this is an investment that works well in their portfolio.

Despite the Adjusted EBITDA warning ET delivered a strong Q2 and I am bullish on their future

This was a strong operational quarter for ET as they achieved several records in the amount of energy transported. ET's midstream gathering volume increased 10% YoY as it reached 21,329 BBtu/d while their crude transportation volume increased 9% YoY to 7,049 MBbls/d even though they took a hit with the Bakken pipeline system. NGL transportation volume increased 4% YoY while their NGL and refined products terminal volumes grew by 3% to 1,553 MBbls/d. ET also experienced a 5% YoY growth rate in their NGL exports while fractionated volumes also increased by 5%.

ET's interstate natural gas transportation volume increased by 11% as they experienced higher contracted volumes and increased utilization rates in their major pipeline systems. The expansion provided by ET's newly placed assets also helped their intrastate natural gas transportation volume increase by 8% YoY. I'm expecting the growth to continue as ET successfully placed several key projects into service during Q2.

The Lenorah II processing plant in the Midland Basin which is a 200 million cubic foot per day (MMcf/d) plant is now running at maximum capacity while the Badger processing plant was commissioned in the Delaware Basin and has the capacity for 200 MMcf/d. ET has added 800 MMcf/d of capacity over the past year and these assets have played a crucial role. ET also placed the Nederland Flexport NGL Export Expansion Project into service which added up to 250,000 Bbls/d of total NGL export capacity.

ET's Q2 results were still strong despite the warning on the earnings call as they generated $3.87 billion in Adjusted EBITDA which was an increase of $11 million YoY. ET's generated $1.96 billion in DCF and allocated $2 billion in organic growth capital in the first half of 2025 which was allocated toward their NGL, refined products, midstream, and intrastate segments. In Q2 ET allocated $1.04 billion toward growth projects while spending $253 million on maintence.

ET updated its guidance to come in at or below the low end of its Adjusted EBITDA guidance of $16.1 billion to $16.5 billion due to continued weakness in the Bakken region. Despite this minor headwind ET is still expected to spend approximately $5 billion on organic growth capital projects in 2025 and they increased the quarterly distribution to $0.33. ET is managing a diversified portfolio of assets where 40% of its Adjusted EBITDA is derived from its natural gas-related assets and no individual segment contributed more than 1/3rd of their Adjusted EBITDA. As more projects are placed into service ET's Adjusted EBITDA and DCF should continue increasing over time and this period of weaker than expected Adjusted EBITDA will pass.

ET announced some new projects in Q2 which should positively impact their financials in the future. The Desert Southwest pipeline project is a 516-mile natural gas pipeline that will provide approximately 1.5 Bcf/d of transportation capacity from the Permian Basin to Phoenix Arizona. ET is projected to spend $5.3 billion on this project and it is expected to be placed into service by Q4 2029. While this may seem like a large investment the project is backed by significant long-term commitments from investment-grade counterparties and ET is planning an open season to sell the remaining capacity that isn't currently contracted.

The Hugh Brinson Pipeline received a positive Final Investment Decision for Phase 2 and investors can expect that phase 1 will be placed into service during the 2026 fiscal year. This pipeline is expected to provide approximately 1.5 Bcf per day of natural gas takeaway from the Permian and connect shippers to ET's intrastate natural gas pipeline network. ET is expected to allocate $2.7 billion between both phases and hey will add bidirectional takeaway capacity which will consist of 2.2 Bcf per day from west to east and 1 Bcf per day from east to west. ET already has 2.2 Bcf contracted from this project.

ET approved the looping of the Lone Star Express Pipeline which will add 150,000 Bbls/d of NGLs from the Northern Delaware Basin, and they signed a Heads of Agreement contract with MidOcean Energy for joint development in the Lake Charles LNG project. ET is able to organically scale at a larger rate than many other midstream operators which will be a net positive benefit in the coming years as the demand for energy continues to grow.

ET still looks undervalued compared to its peers

I compared ET to Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), MPLX LP (MPLX), and Plains All American (PAA) and it still looks undervalued. ET is trading at an enterprise value to Adjusted EBITDA multiple of 8.59x, which is the lowest in the peer group and well under the peer group average of 9.58x. When I look at ET on a market cap to Adjusted EBITDA multiple it trades at 3.77x, which is also the lowest in the peer group. The peer group average is also 5.64x which is 49.34% higher than ET's multiple. ET is still trading at a large discount to its Adjusted EBITDA, which is an opportunity for long-term investors.

The other valuation metric that I look at is the market cap to DCF multiple because I want to pay a great price for the DCF it's generating. ET trades at 6.74x its DCF, which is the 2nd lowest multiple in the peer group and well under the peer group average of 7.71x. Investors are also getting a strong distribution yield at 7.59% with ET which is just under the peer group average of 7.66%. I think ET is trading at a great multiple considering where its peers are being valued and how large of a distribution its operations are generating.

Conclusion

The EIA is projecting that global upstream production and consumption of liquid fuels will increase through the end of 2026 in their short-term outlook which was published in July. This earnings season has been very bullish for ET and other midstream operators as all the Mega caps either maintained or raised their Capex guidance for data centers. ET is in a position where their able to allocate a tremendous amount of capital on organic growth projects that will seamlessly augment their current operations and connect upstream producers to their desired markets. ET is building one of the strongest energy infrastructure companies that can't be replicated. In an environment where the demand and production of energy is expected to increase and new competitors can't compete, I think that ET is being drastically undervalued based on the current and future potential profitability. I think that the current headwinds from slightly lower Adjusted EBITDA in 2025 is being blown out of proportion and this is a great opportunity for long-term investors. As the Fed lowers interest rates ET should get some reprieve on its current debt load and the lower cost of capital could put them in a position where margins expand due to lower interest expenses. I plan on adding to my position as I believe ET should be trading north of $20 per unit.

data.title: EDF: Strong Recent Performance, But Limited Local Currency Exposure A Weakness
data.description:
The Virtus Stone Harbor Emerging Markets Income Fund (NYSE:EDF) is a very popular closed-end fund among those investors who are seeking to earn a high level of income from the assets that they possess. There is a very good reason for this, as this fund is one of the highest-yielding ones in the market today. As of the time of writing, the Virtus Stone Harbor Emerging Markets Income Fund boasts a 14.12% yield, which is substantially higher than any of the major domestic or global fixed-income indices:

Index/ETF

Current Yield

Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index (AGG)

3.83%

Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index (JNK)

6.56%

Vanguard Total World Bond ETF (BNDW)

4.02%

Vanguard Total International Bond Index Fund ETF Shares (BNDX)

4.32%

J.P. Morgan EMBI Global Core Index (EMB)

5.12%

As has been the case for a while now, the highest-yielding of the major domestic and global fixed-income indices that American investors will likely be interested in is the Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index, which tracks junk bonds issued by American companies. With a trailing twelve-month yield of 6.56%, this index has a yield that many income seekers will likely find attractive. After all, interest rates in general have been incredibly low since the start of the 21st century, and it has not been easy to find any asset yielding much above 5% during most of the past twenty-five years. As I pointed out in a previous article, though, the current yield of the junk bond index is not much higher than the level of the federal funds rate during the economic boom of the 1990s. The fact that so many people consider a 6.56% yield to be “high” just goes to show you how much our economic actors have become addicted to low interest rates in recent years.

The Virtus Stone Harbor Emerging Markets Income Fund does much better than even the domestic junk bond index, as we can clearly see from its yield. At the fund’s current yield of 14.12%, the amount of income that it provides to its investors is more than double that of the same amount of money invested in the junk bond index. We can see this by imagining a hypothetical $1 million portfolio. If that portfolio were to be entirely invested in an index fund tracking the Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index, it would generate an annual income of approximately $65,600. This is not too bad, and indeed, when combined with Social Security, it would probably be enough for a retiree to live a reasonably comfortable life. The same amount of money invested in the Virtus Stone Harbor Emerging Markets Income Fund would produce an annual income of $141,200, or about $11,766.67 per month. That is certainly enough to live a very comfortable retirement, and it would probably be enough to cover all of an individual’s bills and still leave money left over to allow them to have some fun in their golden years. Many individuals whom I have spoken to list the desire to pursue their hobbies and interests as a key reason for retiring, and the income provided by this fund could go a long way towards achieving that goal.

Peer Comparison

As we just saw, the Virtus Stone Harbor Emerging Markets Income Fund has a remarkably high yield when compared to any of the fixed-income indices. The fund’s yield is also reasonably attractive when compared to that of its peers, but the relative difference between this fund’s yield and that of other emerging market closed-end bond funds is much narrower than the difference between the Virtus Stone Harbor Emerging Markets Income Fund and the indices. We can see that in this chart:

Fund Name

Morningstar Classification

Current Yield

Virtus Stone Harbor Emerging Markets Income Fund

Fixed Income – Taxable-Emerging Market Income

14.12%

Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund (MSD)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Emerging Market Income

10.20%

Templeton Emerging Markets Income Fund (TEI)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Emerging Market Income

9.34%

Western Asset Emerging Markets Debt Fund (EMD)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Emerging Market Income

10.99%

Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Domestic Debt Fund (EDD)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Emerging Market Income

8.99%

The highest-yielding fund amongst this peer group is the Virtus Stone Harbor Emerging Markets Income Fund. Indeed, the difference between this fund’s yield and the yield of the next-highest-yielding fund (the Western Asset Emerging Markets Debt Fund) is quite stark, representing a full 313 basis points of excess yield. On a $1 million investment, that is an extra $31,300 in annual income, which certainly might be enough to sway many investors in favor of the Virtus Stone Harbor Emerging Markets Income Fund. Of course, we should never buy an asset based solely on its yield, as many times a very high yield relative to other similar funds can be a sign that the market expects a distribution cut in the future. The wisdom of the markets is rarely wrong in this respect either, as most very high-yielding equities or funds do end up cutting their dividends or distributions at some point. This suggests that we may want to have a closer look at this fund’s positioning, holdings, and finances before buying its shares.

As we have seen in various previous articles, many funds with very high yields have been sustaining their distributions by making destructive distributions and destroying their net asset values in the process. Basically, the strategy is to pay out more money than the portfolio earns in investment profits and then repeatedly issue new shares to bring in additional money from new investors that then gets paid out again through the distributions. The Cornerstone funds are somewhat notorious for using this strategy, but they are hardly alone in pursuing it. Many bond funds have been using this strategy to maintain their distributions over the past few years. For example, in a recent article, I showed how the Western Asset High Income Fund II (HIX) has been repeatedly failing to cover its distribution and destroying its net asset value in the process. Despite its very high relative yield, however, the Virtus Stone Harbor Emerging Markets Income Fund does not appear to be doing this. In fact, as we can see here, this fund’s net asset value per share is up 16.27% over the past three years:

This chart shows the fund’s net asset value per share over the trailing three-year period. As the fund’s net asset value is up, this means that the fund’s investment portfolio delivered sufficient investment profits to cover all of the distributions that the fund paid out to its shareholders and still leave the fund with a lot of investment profits that have yet to be distributed to the investors. This is certainly a positive sign for anyone who was hoping to earn a sustainable income from the fund, as the fact that it has fully covered its payout over the past three years suggests that it does not need to cut the payout.

With that said, the majority of these funds have managed to fully cover the distributions that they have made over the past three years. In fact, the only one that did not is the Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund (and even it only slightly missed):

The black line shows the net asset value per share of the Virtus Stone Harbor Emerging Markets Income Fund. The remainder of the funds can be identified by this key:

Other than the Virtus Stone Harbor Emerging Markets Income Fund, the best-performing asset was the Templeton Emerging Markets Income Fund, followed by the Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Domestic Debt Fund. All three of these funds have done exceptionally well over the past few months, which is not surprising when we consider that all three of them have exposure to local currency bonds.

There are two basic types of bonds issued by emerging market governments, and some corporations. A “local currency” bond refers to a bond that is denominated in the currency of the emerging market itself. The issuing entity borrows its own currency from investors and then pays them back when the bond matures. These basically work the same way as U.S. Treasuries, as the government is borrowing in its own currency. Some, if not many, investors are not particularly fond of these bonds because some emerging market nations have basically printed currency to pay off their debts, leaving bond investors with negative real returns. In order to assuage the fears that a similar scenario will occur in the future, some emerging market nations issue bonds denominated in U.S. dollars, euros, or another similar currency. These are called “hard currency bonds” or “international bonds.” These effectively transfer the currency risk from the investor to the issuing nation, but they also require that the emerging market nation be able to acquire whatever currency the bonds are denominated in just to make payments on its debts. As I stated in my last article on the Virtus Stone Harbor Emerging Markets Income Fund, the recent adjustments to international trade policies by the United States will likely make it more difficult for emerging market nations to acquire U.S. dollars to pay their U.S. dollar-denominated debt. This could raise the default risk on many hard currency bonds, but it will not likely have any significant impact on bonds denominated in a currency other than the U.S. dollar.

This could be something that puts this fund at a disadvantage against the Templeton Emerging Markets Income Fund and the Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Domestic Debt Fund. As I showed in two recent articles (see here and here), those two funds invest primarily in local currency bonds issued by emerging market nations. In fact, the website for the Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Domestic Debt Fund explicitly states that it tries to avoid buying any U.S. dollar-denominated debt. This is not the case for the Virtus Stone Harbor Emerging Markets Income Fund, which we can see in a chart found on the website for this fund:

This chart states that 63.75% of the fund’s assets are invested in hard currency bonds. This does not necessarily mean that they are U.S. dollar-denominated bonds, as hard currency bonds can be denominated in euros, British pounds, Japanese yen, or a similar currency of a developed-market nation. However, historically, the majority of hard currency bonds are indeed denominated in U.S. dollars.

A look at the fund’s most recent schedule of investments in its semi-annual report confirms that the majority of the hard currency bonds found in the fund’s portfolio are indeed U.S. dollar-denominated bonds. The entire listing contains too many bonds to show in a single screenshot (click on the link for a full list of the fund’s holdings), but here is a brief sample:

We immediately see that nearly all of these bonds are denominated in either an emerging market currency or in U.S. dollars. There are a handful of euro-denominated bonds, but these are by far the minority of the developed market currencies found in the fund’s portfolio. We see a similar pattern if we look over the rest of the portfolio. In short, the majority of this fund’s holdings appear to be in U.S. dollar-denominated securities.

The reason why this puts this fund at a disadvantage compared to some of its peers is that the U.S. dollar has been losing a lot of value against most foreign currencies since the start of the year. The U.S. dollar index (DXY), which measures the value of the U.S. dollar against a collection of six foreign currencies, is down 9.72% year-to-date:

It is almost certain that the U.S. dollar will continue to weaken if the Federal Reserve does indeed resume interest rate cuts next month. The latest inflation report says that the Core Consumer Price Index is up 3.1% year-over-year and has been getting worse over the past few months:

It is very difficult to make the case that inflation is improving, and indeed, some signs are actually pointing to stagflation. The point here is that it is very hard to believe that inflation will go down if the Federal Reserve cuts interest rates (barring a major recession). Indeed, it will probably get worse, which can be very problematic when we consider the market’s predictions about where the federal funds rate will be at the end of this year:

We can see here that the market believes that the federal funds rate will be in the 350 to 400 range by the end of the year. That would put the real rate of interest at under 1%, assuming that inflation remains stable (which seems unlikely). It gets even worse if we look at the market’s projections for the federal funds rate next year. Here are the probabilities given the current expectations among rates traders at the Chicago Mercantile Exchange:

As of today, we see that the market is currently predicting that the federal funds target rate will be in the 275 to 350 range by the end of next year. That is very close to putting the real rate of interest at zero or negative, unless the country experiences a slowdown in inflation. This does not seem at all likely outside of a major recession, so if the market is right, then the central bank may be about to take the nation back to the era of negative real rates. While that would probably be fine for anyone invested in gold or other real assets, it would be devastating for investors in U.S. dollar-denominated fixed-income securities because it effectively guarantees that they will lose purchasing power over the lifetime of their assets. Non-U.S. dollar-denominated bonds would have a significant advantage in such an environment as long as the foreign currency holds its value. There are no guarantees that this will be the case, particularly given the propensity that some countries have for devaluing their currencies whenever the U.S. dollar falls, but it still suggests that investors should want to diversify their portfolios across currencies. The Virtus Stone Harbor Emerging Markets Income Fund does not do this as well as peers such as the Templeton Emerging Markets Income Fund and the Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Domestic Debt Fund.

Recent Performance

My most recent article on the Virtus Stone Harbor Emerging Markets Income Fund was published on April 25, 2025. The U.S. dollar index is down 1.75% since that time:

In addition to this, inflation figures have generally worsened (the headline inflation rate was +2.3% and Core CPI was only +2.8% in April 2025 compared to 2.7% and 3.1% respectively in the most recent report), but the market has become more convinced than ever that the Federal Reserve is about to start reducing interest rates fairly rapidly. This has, perhaps surprisingly, not had a significant impact on bond yields or bond prices (indeed, twenty-year and thirty-year U.S. Treasury yields are higher today than they were back on April 25), which is probably because the bond market realizes that interest rate cuts will make inflation worse and that the U.S. dollar will have lost a lot of purchasing power by the time that the bonds mature. This has generally been a positive thing for non-U.S. dollar-denominated bonds, which benefit from the weakening currency. U.S. dollar-denominated bonds are up as well, with emerging market bonds and junk bonds doing much better than investment-grade securities:

There is certainly a risk that investors in all three types of U.S. dollar-denominated bonds will end up disappointed here. The fact that U.S. dollar-denominated emerging market bonds are not issued by American entities does not mean that they will do any better than domestic junk bonds at protecting investors from a U.S. dollar devaluation. Those bonds still pay back their investors in U.S. dollars, and if those U.S. dollars are worth less than they were at the time of issuance then investors in them will still lose purchasing power. Nevertheless, for now, the market is bidding up bond prices so there has been the potential for the Virtus Stone Harbor Emerging Markets Income Fund to have earned some trading profits or capital appreciation from these securities.

Overall, then, we might expect the Virtus Stone Harbor Emerging Markets Income Fund to have delivered a reasonably solid performance since our last discussion on it. This is indeed the case, as the fund’s shares have appreciated by 4.40%:

This is not really much better than the performance of the J.P. Morgan EMBI Global Core Index, which tracks the performance of U.S. dollar-denominated bonds issued by emerging market nations, over the period in question. We can see too that this fund exhibited far more volatility than any of the indices, with much greater differences between the peaks and troughs on the chart. We generally expect a leveraged closed-end fund to be far more volatile than indices, though, so this share price volatility is not particularly surprising.

As is usually the case with closed-end funds, the Virtus Stone Harbor Emerging Markets Income Fund did much better on a total return basis than it did in terms of share price performance. We can see that here:

As we can see, investors in the Virtus Stone Harbor Emerging Markets Income Fund earned a 9.48% total return since the last time that we discussed this fund. That is far better than any of the three bond indices delivered, even when we consider their yields in the calculation. This is also not particularly surprising, considering that the Virtus Stone Harbor Emerging Markets Income Fund has a significantly higher yield than any of the bond indices, and bonds primarily deliver their returns through direct payments made to their investors.

About The Fund

According to the fund’s website, the Virtus Stone Harbor Emerging Markets Income Fund has the primary objective of providing its investors with a very high level of total return. Regular readers will likely recognize that I have been somewhat critical of bond funds trying to achieve total return in the past because bonds do not deliver any net capital gains over their lifetimes. Investors merely pay the face value to purchase the bond when it is issued and then receive the face value back when the bond matures. The only investment returns that these securities provide over their lifetimes are the coupon payments that they make to their investors.

The Virtus Stone Harbor Emerging Markets Income Fund could be an exception to this, though, because it invests in both U.S. dollar-denominated bonds and bonds denominated in foreign currencies. Foreign currency bonds can deliver capital gains over their lifetime if the foreign currency in which the bond is denominated gains value against the investor’s own currency (or the U.S. dollar, in the case of this fund). As such, the fund’s total return objective is reasonable here.

With that said, though, investors in the Virtus Stone Harbor Emerging Markets Income Fund have received all of their total investment returns through the fund’s distributions over the past decade. We can see that in this chart:

As we can see, the share price performance of this fund has been very disappointing over the past decade. After all, the price of the fund has fallen by a whopping 60.21%, which makes this one of the worst-performing emerging market debt funds over the period. As shown here, all of its peers have done much better (although all have experienced price declines):

However, we also see that the Virtus Stone Harbor Emerging Markets Income Fund has delivered a 73.06% total return over the past ten years. This simply means that the distributions that the fund paid out over the period were sufficient to completely offset the price decline and still provide the investors with a positive return. It also means that all of the returns that this fund delivered over the period came in the form of income that was provided to the investors and not capital appreciation (although the distributions themselves may have included some capital gains that the fund earned).

It also, perhaps unfortunately, means that investors in this fund have had to reinvest some of the distributions that they receive just to avoid the destruction of capital caused by the share price decline. One of the most important things for income investors is to maintain their overall capital level, if not continue to grow it over time. This is because the consistent destruction of capital means that the investor will eventually run out of money. In order to maintain a stable capital value with the Virtus Stone Harbor Emerging Markets Income Fund, an investor would have needed to reinvest some of the distributions that they received over the past ten years. This basically has the effect of lowering the yield since only some of the income that the fund pays out is actually income that can be spent on the investor’s lifestyle. The rest needs to be reinvested into buying additional shares of the fund. This is not necessarily the end of the world, as the fund still provides more income than would a fixed-income index fund even if half of each distribution is reinvested, but it is something to keep in mind before leaping at the fund’s very high headline yield.

Distribution Analysis

The primary objective of the Virtus Stone Harbor Emerging Markets Income Fund is to provide its investors with a high level of total return. However, as we just saw, this fund has historically delivered the bulk of its investment returns via distributions that it pays to its investors. The Virtus Stone Harbor Emerging Markets Income Fund has paid a distribution of $0.06 per share monthly ($0.72 per share annually) since November 2021. Prior to that, the fund was paying a $0.07 per share monthly distribution, so the most recent action was a distribution cut. In fact, all of the distribution changes that this fund has made over its history have been cuts. The Virtus Stone Harbor Emerging Markets Income Fund has never once increased its distribution, which is problematic given that this means that, due to inflation, the purchasing power of the fund’s distribution has decreased significantly over time. This is not an ideal situation for anyone who is trying to use the distribution to pay their bills or support themselves, but it can also be largely offset by reinvesting the distribution (although that also reduces the spendable income that the fund provides).

Let us take a moment to see how sustainable the fund’s current distribution is. After all, we do not really want to be the victims of another distribution cut. For this purpose, we will look at the fund’s semi-annual report for the six-month period that ended on May 31, 2025. This is a much newer report than the one that was available to us the last time that we discussed this fund, so it should work pretty well as an update.

For the six-month period that ended on May 31, 2025, the Virtus Stone Harbor Emerging Markets Income Fund reported a net investment income of $7.394 million. This was not sufficient to cover the $10.816 million that the fund paid out in distributions over the period.

Fortunately, the Virtus Stone Harbor Emerging Markets Income Fund was able to make up for the difference through capital gains. For the six-month period that ended on May 31, 2025, the fund reported net realized gains of $3.597 million, which were partially offset by $2.950 million in net unrealized losses. While this means that the fund’s investment operations only generated $8.041 million overall (not enough to cover the distribution), the fund did manage to fully cover the payout through net investment income and net realized gains. The total of these two figures was $10.991 million, which was just above the $10.816 million that was paid out in distributions. The fund only failed to cover the distribution due to net unrealized losses, but, of course, unrealized losses can be erased by strength in the market.

Overall, then, there does not seem to be any real reason to worry about the fund’s distribution right now. I will admit that the fund’s net investment income and net realized gains do not provide much excess coverage for the distribution, which is disappointing, but overall, the fund did realize enough profits from its portfolio to cover the distributions.

Valuation

Shares of the Virtus Stone Harbor Emerging Markets Income Fund are currently trading at a 5.14% premium on net asset value. This is a slightly better price than the 5.85% premium on net asset value that the shares of this fund have had over the past month.

Historically, the Virtus Stone Harbor Emerging Markets Income Fund has traded at a premium. It is the only emerging markets bond fund that has this characteristic. The current price is actually a bit better than its historical price, though. Over the past three years, the shares of this fund have traded at prices ranging from an 11.47% discount to a 23.14% premium on net asset value. The average price over the trailing three-year period was a 6.57% premium on net asset value, which is a bit higher than today’s price. Thus, this fund is at a better price than it has had on average over the past few years, but it is still trading at a level that is well above the actual value of its underlying assets.

Here is how the price of this fund compares to that of its peers:

Fund Name

Premium/(Discount)

Virtus Stone Harbor Emerging Markets Income Fund

5.14%

Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Debt Fund

(3.77%)

Templeton Emerging Markets Income Fund

(7.21%)

Western Asset Emerging Markets Debt Fund

(6.11%)

Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Domestic Debt Fund

(4.82%)

As expected, the Virtus Stone Harbor Emerging Markets Income Fund is currently far more expensive than its peers, as it is the only fund that is trading at a price that is higher than the value of the assets backing each of the shares. This could suggest that the fund is overpriced right now, but we should note that the fund is still cheaper than its historical average. Personally, I do not like to pay a premium for any closed-end fund, but some investors might be okay with it.

Expenses

The fund’s semi-annual report states that the Virtus Stone Harbor Emerging Markets Income Fund had an annualized expense ratio of 3.23% for the first half of its current fiscal year. As is the case with most closed-end funds, a significant proportion of this fund’s expenses comes from the interest that it has to pay on the money that it has borrowed in order to obtain leverage. If we exclude the fund’s interest expenses, its annualized expense ratio drops to 1.60% for the first six months of the fiscal year.

Conclusion

In conclusion, emerging markets may be one of the better locations for fixed-income investors to focus their attention in the coming years. This is a pivot from the last fifteen years or so that saw the United States outperform in pretty much all aspects. One reason for this is that the incredibly poor fiscal outlook for the Federal government’s finances makes it highly likely that the Federal Reserve will keep interest rates at lower levels than it should, causing inflation at home and destroying the U.S. dollar’s value. Emerging market currencies are positioned to outperform, as their budgets are closer to being balanced, most of them have positive balances of trade, and their economies are growing at a more rapid pace than those in the developed world. This is good for emerging market local currency bonds, but the Virtus Stone Harbor Emerging Markets Income Fund is primarily invested in U.S. dollar-denominated emerging market bonds. It is hard to see how those will do as well since they do not have the benefit of the appreciating currency. This might result in this fund being at a disadvantage compared to a fund that is more heavily invested in local currency bonds, such as the Templeton Emerging Markets Income Fund or the Morgan Stanley Emerging Markets Domestic Debt Fund.

data.title: CoreWeave, Inc. 2025 Q2 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by CoreWeave, Inc. in conjunction with their 2025 Q2 earnings call.

data.title: Weibo's Q2 Earnings Review: The AI Future Shines, But Q3 Looks Grim
data.description:
Why Did Weibo Stock Soar Yesterday?

Weibo Corporation (NASDAQ:WB) is a $2.6 billion market cap firm, specializing in building a social media platform to distribute content in China, mainly operating through 2 business segments: Advertising and Marketing Services (AMS), and Value-Added Services (VAS).

The WB stock, which trades on the NASDAQ exchange, has been lagging the performance of its close benchmark - KraneShares CSI China Internet ETF (KWEB) - for the past few years, as it can be seen in the YCharts data:

But yesterday, we saw a huge surge in WB stock price (+11.28%) amid massive volumes - and that happened against the backdrop of falling KWEB (-2.68%). So, at least on a YTD basis, WB stock finally started to outperform its benchmark:

It was all thanks to a stronger-than-expected Q2 FY2025 report, which came out yesterday, showing a 1.24% beat on the top line and a much larger beat of 26.22% on the bottom line.

Reviewing Weibo's Q2 FY2025 Results

On a YoY basis, the consolidated revenues went up by just 2%, but the strength in advertising demand from the Chinese e-commerce and automotive sectors, also with promising (although still quite early) results from WB's strategic integration of AI into its core digital offerings, led to a 12.5% YoY increase in EPS, which came as a big surprise to what Wall Streeters were initially modeling out. As the management team added on the call, the e-commerce vertical saw a great boost thanks to increased ad spending during the major "618" shopping festival held in June 2025. Another growing vertical - the Chinese EV automakers - still tends to increase their ad spending on Weibo as the overall competition in the industry heats up, creating a need for a hub for new model launches and public discussion.

But honestly, I'd be cautious in assuming that the post-earnings stock price surge can be sustainable in the medium term. Yes, the revenue mix shift towards e-commerce and EVs that we're witnessing seems to be offsetting the declines in other verticals so far, but the management's outlook on the call sounded a bit cautious to me. The persistent macro pressures, which lead to an uncertain expectation regarding the online games, luxury goods, and entertainment, and FMCG sub-categories, are what can interrupt the Q2 success flowing to the next quarters of this fiscal year.

Because of a "tough year-over-year comparison due to the 2024 Summer Olympics" for Q3, the management guided for some YoY-based pressures on the top line (and hence likely the bottom line as well).

So actually, in Q3, we still face a lot of pressure in terms of FMCG because there was the Olympics last year. So for dairy products and other FMCG had some spending cuts in -- especially in Q3. And the other thing is for the cosmetics and luxury goods. Cosmetics have already quite stabilized, but we still face some pressure in Q3. And also the same thing goes for luxury goods.

And then for cell phone and gaming industry, there is huge uncertainty. For cell phone in this year, we do see that compared to the first half of the year, their budget basically stabilized, but their overall sales went down a lot. So if there is no additional national subsidy policies coming out, we still expect some pressure in terms of the cell phone industry in the latter half of the year.

I couldn't find a specific projection on sales/EPS from the management, but we see that although the stock price increased by over 11% yesterday, there was 1 analyst downgrading the Q3 consensus numbers:

Overall, on a larger scale, the earnings revisions trend that WB is going through doesn't seem to be healthy as well, as the FY2026 EPS consensus fell from $2.87 in mid-2023 to $1.81 as of today (i.e., it dropped by almost 37% in 2 years).

I concur that the progress in AI further implementation into Weibo's "intelligent search", which is already showing promising signs on the unit economics metrics such as MAU (it surpassed 50 million in June), is what can lead to massive earnings consensus changes over time. But with the management's cautious commentary on the most recent earnings call, I think it's prudent to wait out the next couple of quarters before WB can become a nice buying opportunity. The firm holds ~$2.11 billion in cash and short-term investment balance, so it can weather possible earnings fluctuations over the next months.

Now turning to the stock's valuation, it's hard to ignore its mid-single-digit P/E ratios for years to come. But again, at today's consensus, there should come no earnings growth at those P/Es, and so it makes WB look like a value trap.

I think that once the negative YoY comparison is gone, and if we see that Weibo continues to improve the key unit economics metrics like MAU and DAU, investors, who are OK with buying and holding Chinese stocks, can start to consider WB as a nice GARP opportunity to diversify their portfolios.

After Q3 and likely Q4, I think all interested prospective investors should monitor WB's strategic shift towards "interest-based" content feed over the traditional relationship-based, as it seems to be a higher-margin revenue-generating source in the social media industry. It theoretically increases the consumption depth and boosts engagement, which is essential in retaining and attracting new advertisers to the platform. The Q2 results have already shown the early innings of this approach, but I think we need to wait out the Q3 blow from the e-commerce vertical.

Final Thoughts

Although I don't follow Weibo and it was my first time assessing the stock, I generally liked the firm's Q2 print and the progress that has been made to increase the metrics driving the business success. But I'm a bit spooked by the Q3 guided pessimism, so to speak, and I think that those who overlooked it in the management's words risk seeing a meaningful correction after the post-Q2 surge.

Anyway, if the management can sustain the underlying AI-driven improvements in unit economics metrics, Weibo is likely to become a higher-margin business over time, and the current consensus estimates for FY2026-2027 (and likely beyond) will turn out to be plain wrong (i.e., too pessimistic). In this case, WB's mid-single-digit forward P/Es will have to be re-priced to the upside, and the sky would seem like a limit.

With all that said, anticipating some challenges in Q3/Q4, but hoping for better days beyond that, I rate WB as a "Hold". Please let me know what you think about Weibo, and how you would rate it.

Thank you for reading!
